items.subtopic: Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.title: Understanding Varicella and Herpes Zoster: Clinical Syndromes of VZV
items.content: Varicella-zoster virus (VZV) causes two distinct clinical syndromes: varicella, commonly known as chickenpox, which is a benign childhood illness characterized by an exanthematous vesicular rash; and herpes zoster, or shingles, which occurs due to reactivation of latent VZV typically after the sixth decade of life, presenting with a dermatomal vesicular rash and severe pain.
keyPoints: Varicella is usually benign and characterized by an exanthematous vesicular rash in children.
Herpes zoster presents as a painful dermatomal vesicular rash due to VZV reactivation after the sixth decade of life.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.title: Pathogenesis and Transmission of Varicella-Zoster Virus
items.content: VZV transmission occurs primarily via the respiratory route, leading to localized replication in the nasopharynx. This results in viremia, manifesting as diffuse skin lesions. VZV can be detected in blood or lesions using PCR. During primary infection, vesicles involve the corium or dermis with characteristic histopathologic features such as multinucleated giant cells and eosinophilic intranuclear inclusions.
keyPoints: Transmission of VZV is primarily respiratory, leading to nasopharyngeal replication and viremia.
PCR can detect viral DNA in blood or lesions during chickenpox.
Histopathologic features include multinucleated giant cells and eosinophilic intranuclear inclusions.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Epidemiology of Chickenpox: Impact of Vaccination
items.content: Chickenpox, caused by VZV, is highly contagious with a pre-vaccine attack rate of at least 90% among susceptible individuals. The introduction of the chickenpox vaccine has significantly decreased its incidence.
keyPoints: Attack rate before vaccination: at least 90% among seronegative individuals
Historical peak age group for infection: children 5–9 years old (50% of cases)
Vaccination impact: Dramatic decrease in annualized incidence post-1995
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Infectious Period of Chickenpox
items.content: Chickenpox presents with a rash, fever, and malaise. Patients are infectious from ~48 hours before the rash appears until all vesicles have crusted over.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 10 to 21 days (usually 14–17 days)
Infectious period: ~48 h before rash onset, during vesicle formation (~4–5 days), until vesicles are crusted
Fever range in immunocompetent patients: $37.8^{	ext{o}}$C to $39.4^{	ext{o}}$C ($100^{	ext{o}}$F to $103^{	ext{o}}$F)
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Complications of Chickenpox: CNS and Pneumonia
items.content: Chickenpox can lead to complications such as CNS involvement, including aseptic meningitis and encephalitis, and varicella pneumonia, which is more severe in adults.
keyPoints: CNS complications: Acute cerebellar ataxia, meningeal inflammation (~21 days post-rash), lymphocytes and elevated protein in CSF
Encephalitis incidence: 0.1–0.2% of children with chickenpox
Varicella pneumonia: More common in adults (up to 20%), onset 3–5 days into illness, associated symptoms include tachypnea, cough, dyspnea
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.title: Perinatal Varicella Mortality
items.content: Perinatal varicella is associated with high mortality rates due to the lack of protective transplacental antibodies and an immature immune system in newborns.
keyPoints: Mortality rate as high as 30% when maternal disease develops within 5 days before delivery or within 48 hours thereafter.
Newborns are at severe risk because they do not receive protective transplacental antibodies.
Congenital varicella is extremely uncommon.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Herpes Zoster Incidence and Complications
items.content: Herpes zoster incidence peaks in individuals over the sixth decade of life, with complications including postherpetic neuralgia and CNS involvement.
keyPoints: Incidence is highest (5–10 cases per 1000 persons) among those aged 60 and above.
At least 1.2 million cases occur annually in the United States.
Postherpetic neuralgia affects at least 50% of patients over age 50, with pain persisting months after cutaneous resolution.
CNS involvement can lead to symptomatic meningoencephalitis and other neurological manifestations.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Herpes Zoster in Immunocompromised Hosts
items.content: Immunocompromised individuals experience more severe herpes zoster with prolonged lesion formation and increased risk of dissemination.
keyPoints: Lesions continue to form for >1 week, scabbing incomplete until 3 weeks into illness.
~40% of patients with Hodgkin disease or non-Hodgkin lymphoma develop cutaneous dissemination without therapy.
Risk of pneumonitis, meningoencephalitis, hepatitis increases by 5-10% among those with dissemination.
Mortality rate is 10% in hematopoietic stem cell transplant recipients with VZV infection.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.title: Distinguishing Chickenpox from Other Vesicular Rashes
items.content: Chickenpox diagnosis is primarily clinical, based on rash and exposure history. However, other conditions like disseminated HSV in atopic dermatitis or coxsackievirus infections can mimic it but usually present with morbilliform rashes rather than vesicular ones. Rickettsialpox can be differentiated by the presence of a herald spot and more pronounced headache.
keyPoints: Chickenpox diagnosis is clinical, based on rash and exposure history.
Disseminated HSV, coxsackievirus infections present with morbilliform rashes.
Rickettsialpox distinguished by 'herald spot' and severe headache.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.title: Confirming Varicella Zoster Virus Infection with Laboratory Tests
items.content: Definitive diagnosis of VZV infection can be achieved through isolation in cell cultures, seroconversion, or a fourfold rise in antibody titer. PCR detection of VZV DNA is the preferred method due to its high sensitivity and availability in many labs.
keyPoints: Isolation in cell culture lines confirms VZV.
Seroconversion or fourfold rise in antibody titer indicates infection.
PCR for VZV DNA is diagnostic of choice, with specimens including lesions, blood, saliva.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.title: Management of Chickenpox in Immunocompetent Hosts
items.content: Chickenpox management focuses on preventing complications through hygiene and appropriate medication. Antiviral therapy is recommended for adolescents and adults within 24 hours of symptom onset.
keyPoints: Good hygiene includes daily bathing, soaks, and meticulous skin care to prevent secondary bacterial infection.
Pruritus can be managed with tepid water baths, wet compresses, and antipruritic drugs; avoid aspirin due to Reye syndrome risk.

Acyclovir (800 mg orally five times daily), valacyclovir (1 g three times daily), or famciclovir (250 mg three times daily) for 5–7 days is recommended for adolescents and adults with chickenpox onset ≤24 hours.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.title: Oral Antiviral Therapy in Herpes Zoster
items.content: Herpes zoster treatment involves oral antivirals to accelerate healing and alleviate pain. Valacyclovir and famciclovir are preferred due to better pharmacokinetics.
keyPoints: Aluminum acetate soaks can soothe herpes zoster lesions.

Oral antiviral therapy with acyclovir (800 mg five times daily for 7–10 days), valacyclovir (1 g three times daily for 5–7 days), or famciclovir (500 mg three times daily for 7 days) accelerates healing and pain resolution.
Valacyclovir and famciclovir offer less frequent dosing compared to acyclovir.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.title: Management of Herpes Zoster in Immunocompromised Hosts
items.content: Severely immunocompromised individuals require IV acyclovir to reduce visceral complications, with oral therapy for low-risk cases. Corticosteroids may be used cautiously.
keyPoints: IV acyclovir (10 mg/kg every 8 hours for 7 days) is recommended for severely immunocompromised hosts to prevent visceral complications.
Oral valacyclovir or famciclovir can benefit low-risk immunocompromised individuals.
Corticosteroids may be considered by an ophthalmologist for zoster ophthalmicus, but should not replace antiviral therapy.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.title: Vaccination Strategies for Varicella Zoster Virus (VZV) Prevention
items.content: Prevention of VZV infections involves vaccination strategies tailored to different age groups. For children aged 1-12 years and adults who are seronegative, the live attenuated varicella vaccine is recommended with two doses: first at 12–15 months and second at ~4–6 years. For those >13 years old, two doses are administered at least one month apart. Shingrix, a subunit vaccine, is preferred for individuals over 50 to prevent shingles, showing a 97.2% efficacy.
keyPoints: Live attenuated varicella vaccine recommended for children >1 year and seronegative adults
Two doses: first at 12–15 months, second at ~4–6 years; for those >13 years, two doses at least 1 month apart
Shingrix vaccine for individuals >50 with 97.2% efficacy in preventing herpes zoster
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Post-Exposure Prophylaxis and Immune Globulin Use for VZV
items.content: For high-risk individuals exposed to varicella, post-exposure prophylaxis includes administering varicella-zoster immune globulin (VZIG) within 96 hours of exposure, preferably within 72 hours. This is crucial for those ineligible for vaccination or beyond the 96-hour window after contact. Antiviral therapy with acyclovir, valacyclovir, or famciclovir can also be initiated 7 days post-exposure to reduce disease severity.
keyPoints: VZIG administered within 96 hours (preferably 72) of exposure for high-risk individuals
Antiviral therapy with acyclovir, valacyclovir, or famciclovir started 7 days after exposure
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature observed in tissue culture from patients with varicella or herpes zoster?
options: Eosinophilic intranuclear inclusions and multinucleated giant cells
items.explanation: Both varicella and herpes zoster show eosinophilic intranuclear inclusions and multinucleated giant cells in tissue culture.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.question: What is the primary route of transmission for varicella-zoster virus (VZV) during a primary infection?
items.explanation: Primary infection of VZV occurs readily by the respiratory route.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.question: Where does varicella-zoster virus (VZV) remain latent during a primary infection?
items.explanation: During chickenpox, VZV infects dorsal root ganglia where it remains latent.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: Which age group historically had the highest incidence of chickenpox before the introduction of vaccination?
items.explanation: Historically, children 5–9 years old were most commonly affected by chickenpox.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: When are individuals with chickenpox considered infectious?
options: Only after the vesicular rash appears
~48 h before and during the formation of vesicles until all are crushed
items.explanation: Patients with chickenpox are infectious ~48 h before onset of the rash, during vesicle formation, and until all vesicles are crushed.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the most common infectious complication of varicella?
options: Secondary bacterial superinfection of the skin
items.explanation: The most common infectious complication is secondary bacterial superinfection of the skin.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: Which group is at greater risk for visceral complications from chickenpox?
items.explanation: Immunocompromised patients are at greater risk for visceral complications from chickenpox.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.question: What is the mortality rate associated with perinatal varicella when maternal disease develops within 5 days before delivery or within 48 hours thereafter?
items.explanation: Perinatal varicella is associated with mortality rates as high as 30% when maternal disease develops within the specified time frame.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which age group has the highest incidence of herpes zoster?
options: Individuals in their sixth decade and beyond
items.explanation: The incidence of herpes zoster is highest among individuals in the sixth decade of life and beyond.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What percentage of patients over age 50 report some degree of postherpetic neuralgia after herpes zoster?
items.explanation: At least 50% of patients over age 50 report some degree of pain in the involved dermatome for months after the resolution of cutaneous disease.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: What is a rare manifestation of CNS involvement following localized herpes zoster?
items.explanation: A rare manifestation of CNS involvement following localized herpes zoster is granulomatous angitis with contralateral hemiplegia.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following infections can mimic chickenpox but typically presents with morbilliform rashes rather than vesicular or vesiculopustular lesions?
options: Disseminated HSV infection in patients with atopic dermatitis
Coxsackievirus infection, echovirus infection, or atypical measles
items.explanation: Coxsackievirus, echovirus, and atypical measles can mimic chickenpox but typically present with morbilliform rashes.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.question: What is a distinguishing feature of rickettsialpox that helps differentiate it from chickenpox?
options: Vesicular lesions in a dermatomal pattern
Detection of VZV DNA by PCR
Presence of the 'herald spot' at the site of the mite bite
Seroconversion or a fourfold rise in antibody titer
items.explanation: The presence of the 'herald spot' at the site of the mite bite helps differentiate rickettsialpox from chickenpox.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.question: Which diagnostic method is considered the most sensitive for confirming VZV infection?
options: PCR detection of viral DNA in vesicular fluid
items.explanation: PCR detection of viral DNA in vesicular fluid is the most sensitive method for confirming VZV infection.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.question: Which medication is recommended for adolescents and adults with chickenpox of ≤24 hours in duration?
options: Acyclovir (800 mg by mouth five times daily)
items.explanation: Valacyclovir is recommended for adolescents and adults with chickenpox of ≤24 hours in duration.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.question: What should be avoided in children with chickenpox due to the risk of Reye syndrome?
items.explanation: Administration of aspirin should be avoided in children with chickenpox due to its association with Reye syndrome.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.question: Which antiviral therapy is preferred for herpes zoster due to superior pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics?
items.explanation: Valacyclovir and famciclovir are preferred for herpes zoster due to superior pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.question: What is the recommended initial treatment for severely immunocompromised hosts with chickenpox or herpes zoster?
items.explanation: IV acyclovir is recommended at the outset for severely immunocompromised hosts with chickenpox or herpes zoster.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.question: Which age group should receive two doses of the live attenuated varicella vaccine (Oka) at least 1 month apart?
items.explanation: VZV-seronegative persons older than 13 years should receive two doses of the varicella vaccine at least one month apart.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What is the recommended shingles vaccine for individuals aged 50 years or older?
items.explanation: The recommended shingles vaccine for individuals aged 50 years or older is Shingrix.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What is the efficacy of the Shingrix vaccine in preventing herpes zoster virus?
items.explanation: The overall vaccine efficacy for the prevention of herpes zoster virus with Shingrix is 97.2%.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.statement: Herpes zoster is characterized by a diffuse and scattered skin rash.
items.explanation: Herpes zoster presents as a dermatomal vesicular rash, not a diffuse and scattered one.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.statement: VZV is a member of the Herpesviridae family.
items.explanation: VZV shares structural characteristics with other members of the Herpesviridae family.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections | DEFINITION | HISTORY AND ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Chickenpox is more severe in children than in adults.
items.explanation: Varicella pneumonia, a serious complication of chickenpox, occurs more often and is more severe in adults.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Reye syndrome can occur in children treated with aspirin for chickenpox.
items.explanation: Reye syndrome is a historical complication associated with the use of aspirin in children with chickenpox.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.statement: Herpes zoster can result in blindness if the ophthalmic branch of the trigeminal nerve is involved and untreated.
items.explanation: Zoster ophthalmicus, which involves the ophthalmic branch of the trigeminal nerve, can lead to blindness without antiviral therapy.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: Postherpetic neuralgia is common in young individuals.
items.explanation: Postherpetic neuralgia is uncommon in young individuals but more prevalent in those over age 50.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.statement: The lesions of smallpox are all at different stages of evolution at any given time.
items.explanation: Smallpox lesions are all at the same stage of evolution at any given time, unlike chickenpox.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.statement: A Tzanck smear is highly sensitive for detecting VZV.
items.explanation: The sensitivity of a Tzanck smear for detecting VZV is low (~60%).
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS | LABORATORY FINDINGS

items.statement: Aspirin is safe to administer to children with chickenpox.
items.explanation: Aspirin should be avoided in children with chickenpox due to the risk of Reye syndrome.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.statement: Famciclovir is licensed for use in varicella.
items.explanation: Famciclovir is recommended but not licensed for varicella.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.statement: Varicella-zoster immune globulin (VZIG) should be administered within 96 hours of exposure for efficacy.
items.explanation: VZIG is most effective when given within 96 hours, preferably within 72 hours, after significant exposure.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: Antiviral therapy with acyclovir should be started immediately upon exposure to prevent varicella.
items.explanation: Antiviral therapy is initiated 7 days after intense exposure, not immediately upon exposure.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: Chickenpox has an incubation period ranging from 10 to 21 days.
items.reason: The virus is highly contagious and spreads rapidly among susceptible individuals.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain the incubation period.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Secondary bacterial superinfection of skin lesions in chickenpox is usually caused by Staphylococcus aureus.
items.reason: The infection results from excoriation of lesions after scratching.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why secondary bacterial superinfection occurs.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 1)

items.assertion: Herpes zoster can be transmitted to seronegative individuals, causing chickenpox.
items.reason: Patients with herpes zoster can transmit infection to those who have not been exposed to VZV before.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Cutaneous dissemination in herpes zoster is common among immunocompetent individuals.
items.reason: Immunocompromised patients are at greater risk for progressive and disseminated herpes zoster.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; cutaneous dissemination is more common among immunocompromised individuals, not immunocompetent ones.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY AND CLINICAL MANIFESTATIONS (part 2)

items.assertion: Valacyclovir accelerates healing and resolution of zoster-associated pain more promptly than acyclovir.
items.reason: Valacyclovir has superior pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics compared to acyclovir.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections

items.assertion: Glucocorticoids should be used without antiviral therapy in herpes zoster.
items.reason: Glucocorticoid therapy can accelerate quality-of-life improvements when administered early.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; glucocorticoids should not be used without concomitant antiviral therapy.
items.reference: Varicella Zoster Virus Infections · Varicella-Zoster Virus Infections
